sentence: Subjective:
I obtained a detailed history from the patient, Betty, who presented with symptoms of asthma, including persistent coughing and wheezing.
She reported that her asthma symptoms typically worsen when she gets sick with a cold and has also had a flare-up after visiting her aunt's house due to her cat.
She has been using an albuterol inhaler (Ventolin) daily, taking two puffs, and reports that it provides relief but she still experiences symptoms.
She has had multiple visits to urgent care and the emergency department in the past six months, where she was prescribed antibiotics, steroids, and a prednisone taper pack.
Her last exacerbation was about a month ago.
She does not have any history of seasonal allergies or other issues that affect her ability to go outside and engage in activities like hiking.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of asthma, which has been managed with medication and occasional hospitalizations.
She does not have any history of smoking, vaping, or marijuana use.
Review of systems:
The patient does not have any symptoms in other organs that are relevant to her initial presentation.
Current medications:
I prescribed Flovent 110 micrograms, one puff twice per day, for asthma.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 128/82, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 99% on room air.
Physical exam:
The patient's lung exam revealed diminished lung sounds throughout with occasional slight expiratory wheeze bilaterally.
The trachea is midline, and there is no lymphadenopathy.
The patient's hands have strong bilateral pulses, no clubbing, and brisk capillary refill.
Test Results:
The patient's pulmonary function test results are consistent with someone diagnosed with asthma.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are consistent with moderate intermittent asthma.
Problem list: Asthma; confirmed
Plan:
I started the patient on Flovent 110 micrograms, one puff twice per day, to help prevent daily symptoms and reduce her reliance on her albuterol inhaler.
I also instructed her to continue using her albuterol inhaler as needed, with a maximum of two puffs every four to six hours.
I will put together an asthma action plan to help manage her symptoms and identify potential triggers.
I will see her in a month or sooner if her symptoms worsen.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient in a month or sooner if her symptoms worsen.
